summary:
The Amber Road (c. 500–100 BC) was a network of trade routes connecting the amber-producing Baltic coast with the Mediterranean world, one of the oldest and most important long-distance trade networks in European prehistory, through which amber — a fossilized tree resin prized for its beauty and supposed magical properties — traveled thousands of kilometers to grace the courts of Greece, Rome, and Egypt.

Amber from the Baltic coast (modern Poland, Lithuania, and Latvia) was found in Mycenaean Greek tombs and Egyptian royal burials, demonstrating that long-distance contacts existed even earlier than the classical period. During the 500–100 BC period, the routes became more regularized and the trade more substantial. Major routes ran from the Baltic coast southward through central Europe via river systems (Vistula, Oder, Rhine) and mountain passes into the Mediterranean sphere. Amber found in classical Mediterranean contexts is often worked into jewelry, small sculptures, and luxury objects. In return, Mediterranean goods — glass, bronze, wine, pottery — traveled northward along the same routes. The Amber Road's effect was not merely commercial: it transmitted Mediterranean artistic influences northward and brought northern European peoples into the cultural orbit of the classical world centuries before Roman conquest.
